repairs.before: "Soul Makossa" is a song by Cameroonian saxophonist and songwriter Manu Dibango, released as a single in 1972. It is the most sampled African song in history. The song was originally recorded as the B-side for "Hymne de la 8e Coupe d'Afrique des Nations", a song celebrating the Cameroon national football team's accession to the quarterfinals of the Africa Cup of Nations football tournament, as well as Cameroon's hosting the games for the first time; the lyrics were written by Cameroonian poet and musicologist S.M. Eno Belinga. Except for some words in English, it was written in Duala, a native dialect continuum from Cameroon. A makossa-funk landmark and one of the most influential African records in global pop. Michael Jackson's 'Wanna Be Startin' Somethin'' borrowed its chant, creating one of music's first copyright disputes.

One of the most popular Philadelphia soul groups of the 1970s, the group's repertoire included soul, R&B, doo-wop, and disco. Masekela included the song in his albums Grazing in the Grass: The Best of Hugh Masekela (2001), Still Grazing (2004), and Live at the Market Theatre (2006). repairs.before: The Art Ensemble of Chicago is an avant-garde jazz group that grew out of the Association for the Advancement of Creative Musicians (AACM) in the late 1960s. The ensemble integrates many jazz styles and plays many instruments, including "little instruments": bells, bicycle horns, birthday party noisemakers, wind chimes, and various forms of percussion. The musicians would wear costumes and face paint while performing. These characteristics combined to make the ensemble's performances both aural and visual. While playing in Europe in 1969, five hundred instruments were used. Showed the Art Ensemble's range and their ability to work in commercial contexts without compromising. 'Theme de Yoyo' became a rare avant-garde dance floor hit.

repairs.before: Abdullah Ibrahim, previously known as Dollar Brand, is a South African pianist and composer. His music reflects many of the musical influences of his childhood in the multicultural port areas of Cape Town, ranging from traditional African songs to the gospel of the AME Church and Ragas, to more modern jazz and other Western styles. Ibrahim is considered the leading figure in the subgenre of Cape jazz. Within jazz, his music particularly reflects the influence of Thelonious Monk and Duke Ellington. He is known especially for "Mannenberg", a jazz piece that became a notable anti-apartheid anthem. The most important South African jazz recording. 'Mannenberg' became a rallying cry for freedom, played at protests and celebrations.

repairs.before: The best-selling jazz album of all time, Kind of Blue introduced modal jazz to the world. Recorded at Columbia's 30th Street Studio in New York on March 2 and April 22, 1959, the album captures spontaneous creativity at its peak—most tracks were first takes with minimal rehearsal. The legendary sextet featured John Coltrane and Cannonball Adderley on saxophones, pianist Bill Evans (Wynton Kelly on 'Freddie Freeloader'), bassist Paul Chambers, and drummer Jimmy Cobb. Evans contributed the album's modal framework, drawing from impressionist composers like Ravel and his studies with theorist George Russell. Revolutionized jazz by moving away from bebop's complex chord changes to modal improvisation based on scales, opening new harmonic possibilities that influenced countless musicians across genres from rock to hip-hop. It remains the quintessential introduction to jazz, selling over five million copies and consistently ranking among the greatest albums ever recorded in any genre.

repairs.before: John Birks 'Dizzy' Gillespie was a trumpet virtuoso who co-founded bebop with Charlie Parker and became one of jazz's greatest ambassadors. His technical mastery allowed him to execute dazzling runs at breakneck tempos, while his harmonic innovations helped define the bebop language. Beyond bebop, Gillespie was instrumental in developing Afro-Cuban jazz, collaborating with Cuban percussionist Chano Pozo to create groundbreaking fusions like 'Manteca' and 'A Night in Tunisia.' Known for his bent trumpet, puffed cheeks, and ebullient personality, he balanced virtuosity with showmanship and humor. Gillespie mentored countless young musicians and led State Department-sponsored tours that spread jazz worldwide, remaining a vital force in music until his death in 1993.

repairs.before: Ornette Coleman was an alto saxophonist, violinist, and composer who revolutionized jazz by liberating it from chord changes and conventional song forms. His 1959 debut 'The Shape of Jazz to Come' and 1960's 'Free Jazz'—a collective improvisation that gave the movement its name—were initially controversial but proved prophetic. Coleman's self-taught approach and plastic alto saxophone produced a raw, vocal-like tone that divided audiences: some heard noise, others heard pure emotion. His theoretical concept of 'harmolodics'—treating melody, harmony, and rhythm as equal—influenced everyone from Pat Metheny to punk rockers. Coleman continued exploring through electric bands like Prime Time, classical compositions, and collaborations until his death in 2015, remaining one of music's most fearless innovators.

repairs.before: Roy Ayers is a vibraphonist whose jazz-funk innovations with Ubiquity made him one of the most sampled artists in hip-hop history. Starting as a hard bop vibraphonist influenced by Milt Jackson, Ayers pivoted to funk in the 1970s, creating a sound that merged jazz sophistication with danceable grooves. Songs like 'Everybody Loves the Sunshine' and 'Running Away' became hip-hop building blocks, sampled by artists from A Tribe Called Quest to Mary J. Blige. Ayers' warm vibes tone, smooth vocals, and positive energy created a template for contemporary jazz-influenced R&B. Now in his 80s, he continues performing and has seen his influence acknowledged by generations of musicians who grew up on his sampled legacy.

repairs.before: Sonny Rollins is a tenor saxophonist whose massive sound, thematic improvisation, and uncompromising artistic standards have made him jazz's greatest living legend. His 1956 album 'Saxophone Colossus' established him as Coltrane's equal, featuring the calypso 'St. Thomas' which became his signature tune. Famous for his sabbaticals—including a period practicing on the Williamsburg Bridge walkway to avoid disturbing neighbors—Rollins has repeatedly withdrawn from the scene to reassess his music. His improvisations are architectural marvels, building on motifs and developing themes with a logic that connects him to Coleman Hawkins and the saxophone's deepest traditions. Now in his 90s, Rollins remains revered as a musician who never stopped challenging himself.

repairs.before: Terence Blanchard is a trumpeter and composer who has extended the Jazz Messengers tradition while becoming one of Hollywood's most respected film composers. Taking over Art Blakey's trumpet chair alongside fellow New Orleans native Wynton Marsalis, he developed a warm, singing tone and dramatic sensibility that transferred naturally to cinema. His collaboration with Spike Lee has produced over 20 film scores, while his concert works like 'A Tale of God's Will' documented Hurricane Katrina's devastation. Blanchard continues to lead powerful groups, mentor young musicians, and compose music that addresses social justice.
